title: Needle-felted acorn and oak leaf set
subtitle: Three acorns with felted cups and two oak leaves as an autumn grouping
excerpt: Three small needle-felted acorns, each with a textured cup, and two flat oak leaves make a compact autumn grouping. The project introduces felted surface texture (the stippled cup), shape-over-wire construction for the leaves, and multi-piece assembly. Finished size: the largest acorn is about 3 cm long.
sourceNotes: Needle-felted botanical miniature work. Technique is observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A surface texture produced by stabbing the felting needle repeatedly in a small area without attempting to smooth the surface. The result is a bumpy, uneven texture resembling the scales on an acorn cup.

A thin wire frame inside a needle-felted piece that provides a skeleton. Wool is needled around the armature to build up the shape. Armatures allow thin flat shapes (leaves, petals) that would not hold their form in wool alone.

content.text: Each acorn is built from two needle-felted pieces: a smooth oval body in tawny brown, and a stipple-textured cup in grey-brown. The leaves use a
to hold their lobed shape flat. The finished set makes a windowsill display or can be wired into a wreath.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Keep a foam pad under the work at all times; never needle over a hard surface.

items.name: Merino roving, olive green and rust (for leaves)

content.text: For each acorn, form a small ball of tawny roving and needle it into an oval about 2.5 to 3 cm long and 2 cm wide. The body should be firm and smooth, with no visible fibre tufts on the surface. A fine-needle final pass over the surface (using the size-40 needle) produces the characteristic smooth shell.
Step 2: Form and texture the cups
For each cup, form a shallow disc of grey-brown roving, roughly 1.5 cm across. Needle it until it holds shape as a flat disc. Then use the coarse needle (size 36) to
the surface: stab repeatedly in tight clusters across the top face. Do not try to smooth the surface; the uneven texture is the goal.

Cup the disc over the blunt end of the acorn body. Needle around the edge of the cup where it meets the body, working the needle at a shallow angle inward. The fibres from the cup will lock into the acorn body. Check from the side that the cup sits level.

Cut two short lengths of thin wire, each about 7 cm, and bend each into the outline of an oak leaf (lobed silhouette). Lay a thin layer of olive roving on the foam pad. Place the wire outline on top. Cover with another thin layer of roving. Needle through the entire stack, stitching the wire into the wool. Add rust-coloured roving to the tips and needle in for colour.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two common problems with miniature needle-felted pieces.

items.symptom: Cup comes off the acorn body when handled
items.cause: The join between cup and body was not needled deeply enough to pull fibres from one piece into the other.
items.fix: Push the needle at a low angle from the cup edge into the body through at least 5 mm depth, and work around the entire circumference. The join should resist a firm pull.

items.symptom: Leaf wire pokes through the felt surface
items.cause: Insufficient roving over the wire, or needle pushed the wire through the felt rather than through the foam pad.
items.fix: Add a thin wisp of roving over the exposed wire and needle it flat. Ensure the foam pad is thick enough that the needle does not hit the table beneath it.
